sentences.text: Yes.
My name is Robert Geminder.
And--
Sure.
G-E-M-I-N-D-E-R.
And this is September 24th, 1995.
We're in Palos Verdes, California and doing an interview.
It's a good place to start, Bernie.
I was born on August 3rd, 1935 in Wroclaw, Poland.
Actually, in those days it was better known as Breslau.
It was kind of right on the border of Poland and Germany, so it had a German name which was far more popular than the Polish name.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my life-- I'll tell you a little story.
My life kind of started with a real bang.
Because when I was born, my brother who was two years old, was out taking a walk with his maid or with his caretaker.
And I was born.
And when he came in, I was already born.
And I was in a little bed, bassinet of some sort.
And the bang happened was when he slapped me.
And he said malpa, meaning monkey.
I guess I was about as ugly as a monkey when I was born on that August 3rd date.
Incidentally, my Hebrew name was given to me as Yisrael.
Well, I was-- when I was a little kid, of course, my father-- we lived in this apartment complex.
Just to give you a couple of incidents, he was one of these dads that would let the young little kids do just about anything to keep them happy and keep them smiling.
He would open up the apartment doors and we'd ride our bikes through the apartment doors, my little tricycle.
In fact, one time he took us outside, and we-- I had a fairly serious accident.
Rode into a barbed wire fence, which I have a scar somewhere here for that.
But-- so that was some incidents you might say before the war when I was at the age of, say, zero to four.
He also liked to give us candy.
He would line up-- in Europe they had these chocolate candies that are made like little figures.
And inside is some kind of a syrup, you know, like a raspberry or strawberry syrup.
And he would line these up and my brother and I would go through a few of these.
So my memories are very, very pleasant at that time.
My father's name, incidentally, was Meno or Menachim Mendel Geminder.
And I have to at this point say, 'Guh-min-der.' Because actually the name was pronounced 'Guh-min-der' in Europe.
When I came to this country, things were-- I wanted to make things easy.
I didn't want to stand out the most, so you know how it is when you're a greenhorn.

So I was advised to pronounce the name, 'Jeh-mine-der.' Later, of course, I found that many of my cousins living in this country, born in this country, pronounced the name 'Guh-min-der,' the way it should be pronounced.
But we still go by 'Jeh-mine-der.' So that was my father's name.
And my mother's name is Bertha.
And she went by the name of Bertl, B-E-R-T-L.
And she-- she had-- my grandfathers from my father's side were already dead.
But my grandparents from my mother's side were alive.
And they were in the-- on the scene, too.
And so was my mother's brother, an older brother by name of Artur.
Glotzer.
The name was Glotzer.
So my grandfather's name was Ira Glotzer, and my mother's name was Glotzer.
In fact, an interesting story-- of course, I was told this.
I wasn't born yet-- of how my parents met.
Ira Glotzer, my mother's father, met a very nice man, and he asked him to come to a cafe house.
In those days, everybody in the afternoon went to a little cafe house, and they had coffee and ice cream.
And he arranged it so my to-be dad, Meno, was there, and my mother came.
And that's how they were introduced at this coffee place.
It was kind of arranged but not really arranged.
Just the introduction was arranged.
And my father was considerably older than my mother, like 21 years older.
So he was already semi-retired, if you will.
He-- we had a lot of-- he had a lot of apartment buildings in Poland.
And so make a long story short, my mother and he were married in 1931.
My mother had a brother.
My dad had a lot of family.
There were like seven, eight of them.
But they, of course, were all born in Europe in a little area near Krakow call Tarnow, a little city called Tarnow.
But they were already-- a lot of them were leaving Europe.
I don't know the exact dates, but they were leaving.
Some of them went to Brazil.
Some went to Chile.
And some went to the United States.
So my father and just a few of his brothers, a couple of half-brothers were still in Europe.
But basically, on my mother's side, she had one brother.
And I had one brother.
There were only two of us, my brother George, who was two years older than I was.
Well, it's hard for me to recall, you know, history.
I was like three years old in 1938.
But I do recall that the bad stuff started.
You know, let's-- let's face it.
What I recall and what I've been told is very difficult to separate.
But in some cases I recall.
In some cases, I've been told some of these things.
So the bad things really started in our area like in 1938.
We at this point already were forced out of Breslau-- Wroclaw, the city-- in our apartment.
And we had to relocate in-- in Poland, because Germany was already taking pieces of Poland at that time.
So we essentially were relocated, or we moved to another part of Poland.
I don't-- I don't recall, and I'm not sure.
They were very Jewish.
Especially my grandparents, the Glotzers, were very Jewish and kosher.
My father was a little what we'd probably call today on the reform conservative side.
Traditional more than anything.
Whether they belonged to a temple, I don't-- I don't recall.
And I don't-- I certainly don't have any memories of being in a synagogue prior to 1938, from my age of zero to three.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
That was, as I said, in '38.
And we moved into Poland.
Because basically, we were moving east.
The idea was to move east away from the Germans, towards the Russians.
This kind of a move was, you know, you typically-- maybe you've seen it in movies or read in books about it-- it was by wagon.
It was by foot.
There were no trains, at least not for us at that point.
So it was a very difficult move.
Our first move was really to our grandparents' home, the Glotzers, who already lived in Poland.
What was more-- what was also difficult for me, George and I, actually-- that we spoke German.
Our first language was German.
Being born on the border like that, German was the language.
So here we were in Poland, and yet our first language was German.
And needless to say, this was a hated, hated language by Poland, even as early as 1939.
So we-- we quickly tried to learn Polish and we tried to get into the-- into that language.
When you lived with your grandparents, do you recall if there was much in the way of army coming in, soldiers coming in?
No.
It was more like the police at that point.
You know, the war hadn't really started yet officially.

But as you know, they were dividing Poland into pieces at that time, so it was more like the police were-- I don't-- I wanted to say Gestapo, but quite honestly, I don't know if the Gestapo was already in effect at that point or not.
But it was the German police that were doing most of the-- most of the damage.
In other words, it was a time in Poland and in parts we were that you can no longer have meetings.
You could no longer go to a restaurant.
Certainly all the synagogues were no longer open to Jews and so on.
So it was-- it was kind of the beginning.
You could no longer escape.
At least that was not an avenue.
For us, it didn't appear to be an avenue.
And we kept moving, constantly moving towards-- towards-- Russia.
Russia.
Towards the Russian border.
In fact, Poland was kind of divided into two pieces at that point.
Half of it was Russia, the other half was Poland-- it was Germany, I'm sorry.
Germany and Russia.
And we, of course, wanted to be on the Russian side.
So we finally settled in a town called Stanislawow, which incidentally today-- it's so far east that today it's actually part of the Ukraine.
So it's not even Poland.
It's Ukraine.
But in those days, in 1939, it was-- it was Poland.
Yes.
They-- for part of it.
And they settled in a different city then Stanislawow, my grandfather and grandmother.
Where we were in Stanislawow, which was a big city.
And it was still-- we were still pretty much a family unit-- my father, my mother, my brother, and I.
There were some other relatives but basically those.
My grandparents stayed in a little town nearby.
In fact, they wanted the kids-- which is George and me-- but I didn't go.
But my brother went over there for a little while to make it kind of easier for my mother to only have one child at a time.
And now we're into 1940 and pushing 1941.
And this is, of course, when the war really started.
You know, the bombing started happening.
And I recall-- and this I recall very vividly-- being in this little apartment in Stanislawow for-- everybody was running down to the cellar, because there was bombs hitting.
And my father was not feeling well.
He couldn't go down the cellar.
He already had evidently angina or some form of heart-- he was very-- he was quite ill.
So we stayed upstairs in the apartment.
And I remember I was helping even-- my mother got somebody to help us, but I was basically helping to put a big couch up against the window, because you know, so you wouldn't have glass shattered.
Shattering.
Yeah.
You know, if a bomb hit that building, there wasn't much you could do.
But at least if it hit nearby, you didn't have glass.
So we had this big couch up against or bed up against the window kind of closed off.
And we all huddled and stayed together with my father.
And it's at this point that he died.
There was just-- my mother had tried to get a doctor, tried to get medication, tried to get everything.
It was impossible.
So he died pretty much like on-- you might say on the first day of the war for-- it wasn't the first day of the war officially.
But it was the first day of the war--
In Stanislawow when the troops came in.
Incidentally, the first troops that came in to Stanislawow were Hungarian troops, not German troops.
As you know or recall perhaps, Hungary joined Germany in the war.
They were on their side, just like Italy was with the Germans.
So the Hungarians moved into Stanislawow first.
Which was good, because they were not as anti-Semitic.
Jews were not a big issue for the Hungarians as they were for the Germans.
It happened around May, '41.
You know, that's kind of our yahrzeit here, so I didn't look up the exact date.
But it happened around May, '41.
In fact, because the Hungarians were there as opposed to the Germans, we were able to have a funeral.
I mean, this took a lot of doing and perhaps a lot of diamonds on my mother's part.
And that's something I should perhaps mention, in-- because it-- it did play a big part.
I was born a millionaire.
You know, my parents were already very-- my father was very, very wealthy.
So from zero to four years or three years of age, I was a-- I was a very rich-- a rich person.
And my mother had a lot of gold and a lot of diamonds.
And later on, of course, this helped us in many, many ways.
But even then, at the beginning, we were able-- she was able to buy a horse and carriage and have a funeral.
My father was actually buried in a Jewish cemetery without a stone, without anything.
This is like one day after the Hungarians moved in.
No markers, no Jewish markers.
But he was buried properly.
And I remember we went to the-- there was a big issue whether kids should go to the funeral or not.
You know, in Europe, they were very funny in those days about rituals of that type.
But we did go to the funeral, George-- when I say we, I mean my brother and I.
And followed the wagons and actually we buried-- buried my father on that.
It was around May of 1941.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I recall-- just to tell you one thing about my father.
There was only one time-- of course, this was 1941, so I was six years old.
There's only one time that my father ever put a hand on me, so to say, in the six years.
He was a great, great, unbelievable personality person.
A wonderful person.
And I remember in Stanislawow he was-- somehow he found a bottle of ginger ale or soda water.
So he was giving George and me soda water.
And I was complaining to him because he gave my brother a little more than me.
And I guess I started crying.
It was more than just complaining casually.
You know how kids are when they're five or six.
And this is when he got a little angry and slapped me on the tush.
And that's the only time he ever laid a hand on me.
So he was-- I have nothing but fantastic, fond memories of my father.
No.
No, the Hungarians--
No.
The Hungarians were just occupied temporarily, and everyone knew that the Germans were coming.
In fact, we tried even before they came or right at that time to go into Russia.
But unfortunately, the Russians were only interested in males of military age.
In fact, a couple of cousins of mine who survived the war were like 10 years older than me, which would mean at least 16, let's say.
And they were allowed to go into Russia.
But they didn't want any families and certainly no children.
So we were kind of stuck there.
The Hungarians were merely, in this point, in a holding pattern, you know.
The Germans hadn't come yet.
I have no idea how long this lasted, whether this was a week, or three days, or two weeks, you know.
But it was a very short period of time.
And then the Germans came in to Stanislawow.
And just to digress a little bit-- or not digress but I think I mentioned earlier that my grandparents, Ira and Golde Glotzer, were in a different little town outside of Stanislawow.
Well, the Germans came into that town already.
And the Germans in many ways were accompanied by Ukrainians in that part of the world.
The Ukrainians kind of jumped on the German side.
And especially when it came to Jews, they were very, very anti-Jewish, to put it bluntly.
And anyway, they took my grandfather away like in the first few days.
And my mother-- we never really knew what happened to him, except later on we heard stories that he was shot kind of in the street and taken away and shot.
So at this point, my grandmother, Golde, joined us in Stanislawow.
She was all alone, so she came to Stanislawow.
And you asked about the Germans.
Well, once the Germans came in, one of the first things they did-- and we're now talking about October, so maybe it wasn't the first thing.
Again, I don't know the exact months.
But this date certainly stands out in my mind, because it's-- not because I remember the date, but it was a-- one of those horrible events that you know the date of.
October 12th, 1941, the Germans rounded up all the Jews in the city.
And when I say rounded them up, they went through apartments and they literally pushed people out into the center of courts, like town courts.
And there they had trucks.
And they trucked all the Jews-- and we were part of this group-- to a cemetery nearby in-- right outside of-- right in Stanislawow.
And fortunately for us, we got there early.
So that when they put the people in the cemetery, we were-- because we were taken early, we were put way back.
You know, the people that came later were in the front.
Anyway, that day they shot and killed 12,000 Jews of the 15,000 that were at that cemetery.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I can-- again, it's hard to tell whether I remember this or I was told this, but I-- it's a vivid memory, so I must remember it.
Seeing the German eating a sandwich.
I was told later it was a ham sandwich.
And with a machine gun and just shooting.
You know, digging graves.
The people were digging graves.
And shooting them and-- and people were falling into the graves.
In fact, that night, some people that were shot later even were able to crawl out of the graves, because they weren't-- they weren't dead.
They just fell in.
But again, to get back to me and George, we were together.
And my mother was together.
Of course, my dad wasn't there anymore, so my-- it was-- in terms of the family, it was my grandmother, Golde, my mother, and George, and I.
And we were way in the back of the cemetery.
So when they were shooting people and killing people, of course, it was the first batch.
So we were like 3,000 that were left.
We were obviously in the way back.
And then fortunately for us, it started to snow.
And it started to get dark.
So the Germans shouted, everyone who is remaining can go home, you know.
And of course, you can imagine what a stampede this was.
I mean, a tremendous stampede.
Because they wouldn't let the people go forward where they were shooting where the graves were.
They essentially said you can leave going out the back of the cemetery.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at this point, I was separated from my mother.
And you know, it was just like people-- it was like a mass exit.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My grandmother was older, of course.
And she was-- got up.
But after, some of the-- most of the people had left and she was-- she had lost her shoe, so she was looking for her shoe.
And as she looked for her shoe, she saw-- she saw the color of my coat, so she tugged at my coat.
And sure enough, there was me kind of half-dead, half-crushed under people.
So she grabbed me and pulled me out.
And then she figured since she found me, she started looking for George.
And she found him also.
And at this point, you know, as I recall, there was nobody there.
There were just a few people.
There were a lot of people kind of laying on the ground.
And I don't even know if she ever did find her shoe or not.
But now that she had the both of us, she started to move back where we were supposed to exit.
And leave it to the Germans, there was this huge fence you had to climb to exit, you know.
And we looked up at this fence.
It was huge to me.
Don't forget, I was probably about this tall at that point, you know.
But it was really huge to me.
So somehow we got over the fence.
I remember being pushed, being shoved, being put, falling down on the other side.
But basically, George, and I, and my grandmother got over the fence and started walking towards our apartment.
My mother had left.
She was not knocked down like we were.
She was separated.
She didn't want to leave, because she wanted to find the children.
She was convinced by somebody that they said, your children, we saw them.
They were ahead of you.
They're already probably waiting for you at the apartment.
Because you see, everybody was afraid if they didn't immediately leave, you know, we thought, you're sitting there all day seeing 12,000 people shot that you'd be killed, too.
So she believed this person and went back to the apartment.
Of course, we weren't there.
So you can imagine what a tremendous reunion it was when my grandmother brought us in there.
I mean, it was-- so that was, needless to say, if it wasn't for my grandma at that point, there wouldn't be any-- You wouldn't have been there.
There wouldn't be any interview right now.
But this was, of course, the real bad, bad beginning of-- and of course, when we got to the apartment, everything was gone in the apartment, everything.
One thing my mother did, her father told her that to hide all the jewelry, and gold, and diamonds in a bucket.
And he made a bucket for her.
A bucket that you have a false bottom on it, OK.
You know, in Europe, everybody always had a bucket of water next to the fireplace.
And there was this bucket with a false bottom sitting next to the fireplace.
Of course, everything was taken from the apartment, but the bucket was there.
So the bottom of the bucket, which had all this gold and diamonds was intact.
Which, as I-- as we go through the story, you'll see how important later on those things did help.
So that was kind of the bad beginning.
No.
No.
At this point--
Well, at this point, they started-- they-- there was a ghetto set aside.
In fact-- you know, and I looked up some data on this.
Again, this isn't what I remember.
But Stanislawow, the Jewish council in Stanislawow worked out-- and the Germans worked out an area of a ghetto area where the Jews would be.
I think everyone knows what a ghetto is.
You know, it's an area that was totally closed and all the Jews were kind of put in there.
Well, it turns out that the ghetto [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was going to be way too small for all these people.
So that's one reason why they had this mass massacre at the cemetery for that.
But the ghetto was formed and the remaining Jews, which incidentally was more than 3,000 because they didn't find all the Jews taken to the cemetery.
For example, my stepfather-to-be later, he somehow escaped.
He was in hiding and he never went to the cemetery, you see.
In our case, it was a little different.
Because people-- when the Germans came to an apartment, they said, are there any Jews here?
And the Polish would always say-- in most cases, they would point towards the Jews.
